blocks.statement: I can derive FΔt = Δp from F = ma, name FΔt as impulse, and use the equation to find force, time, or change in momentum given the other two.

blocks.whatWeKnow: To CHANGE 2026-XJ's momentum we need to apply a force OVER A TIME.
blocks.connection: Today's equation IS the DART equation. A small spacecraft, hitting an asteroid for a tiny instant, delivers a Δp — same math we use for a bat hitting a ball.

blocks.markdown:
## Where FΔt = Δp comes from

Start with Unit 1's law and unpack the acceleration:

**F = m·a = m·(Δv/Δt)**

Multiply both sides by Δt:

**F·Δt = m·Δv = Δp**

Name F·Δt the **impulse**, J. Then **J = Δp**. Units check: N·s = kg·m/s — impulse and momentum are the same kind of quantity, which is exactly why one can change the other.

terms.definition: The product of force and the time it acts: J = F·Δt. Equals the change in momentum Δp. A 0.002-s bat-hit at 6,750 N delivers J = 13.5 N·s of impulse to the baseball.
terms.cognate: Sp. impulso · Pt. impulso · HC enpilsyon

blocks.title: Impulse is NOT just force
blocks.markdown: It's force × TIME. Half the force for twice the time gives the **same** impulse — and the same Δp.

blocks.title: See it in the simulation
blocks.markdown: Run the impulse-momentum simulation below. Watch the F-vs-t graph during the collision: the AREA under that curve is the impulse, and it always equals the cart's Δp.

blocks.prompt: A 0.15-kg baseball moves east at 40 m/s, then rebounds WEST at 50 m/s. Bat-ball contact lasts 0.002 s. Find (a) Δp, (b) the average force.
blocks.equation: Δp = m·(v_f − v_i) ; F = Δp / Δt
blocks.answer: Δp = −13.5 kg·m/s (westward). F ≈ −6750 N (westward, applied BY the bat ON the ball).

blocks.prompt: DART hit Dimorphos for about 0.05 s with an average force of about 8 × 10⁵ N. What change in asteroid momentum did it deliver?

blocks.title: Two roads to the same J
blocks.markdown: The same J can come from **BIG F over a SHORT Δt** or **SMALL F over a LONG Δt**. That choice is tomorrow's payoff — it's the difference between DART and a gravity tractor.

blocks.prompt: DART hit its target for 0.05 s with an average 8×10⁵ N. What Δp did it deliver to the asteroid? Show your work.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **8.2** (impulse changes momentum; increasing vs. decreasing momentum; extending impact time). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
